name: Almonaster La Real, Santa Ana La Real, Alájar, Castillo de Cortegana – circular route
name_original: Almonaster La Real, Santa Ana La Real, Alájar, Castillo de Cortegana – ruta circular
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Almonaster La Real traverse a small mountainous relief within the Sierra de Aracena y Picos de Aroche Natural Park, offering varied terrain. Cyclists encounter traditional clear wood pastures known as dehesas, dotted with cork and holm oaks, alongside abundant chestnut and pine forests. Valleys are often lined with alders, ash trees, and willow trees, contributing to the scenic diversity. The region features roads with both gentle slopes and challenging climbs, providing panoramic views of the surrounding Sierra. Best road cycling routes around Almonaster La Real The most popular road cycling route is Almonaster la Real, Encinasola, Barrancos, Aroche, Cortegana – circular route, a 67.6 miles (108.9 km) trail that takes about 5 hours 45 minutes to complete. This difficult route connects several towns with significant elevation gain. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Almonaster La Real, Santa Ana La Real, Alájar, Castillo de Cortegana – circular route, a difficult 51.8 miles (83.4 km) path. This route winds through varied landscapes, offering views of the Sierra and its historic sites. Local road cyclists also love the Circular route through Valdemusa, El Cerro de, a 54.2 miles (87.2 km) trail leading through varied terrain and offering expansive views, often completed in about 4 hours 13 minutes. Road cycling around Almonaster La Real is defined by small mountainous relief, traditional dehesas, and dense chestnut forests. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult ability levels. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 300 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Almonaster La Real's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Almonaster La Real?
A: You'll find nearly 50 road cycling routes around Almonaster La Real, catering to various skill levels. These include 2 easy routes, 21 moderate routes, and 24 challenging routes, offering diverse experiences across the region's unique landscape.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes in Almonaster La Real?
A: The terrain around Almonaster La Real is characterized by a 'small mountainous relief' within the Sierra de Aracena y Picos de Aroche Natural Park. You'll cycle through traditional clear wood pastures known as 'dehesas' with cork and holm oaks, abundant chestnut forests, and valleys lined with alders, ash, and willow trees. Routes feature a mix of gentle slopes and demanding climbs, often on serene roads with minimal traffic, providing spectacular views.

Q: Are there road cycling routes suitable for beginners or those looking for an easier ride?
A: Yes, Almonaster La Real offers options for all abilities. While many routes involve significant elevation, there are 2 easy and 21 moderate road cycling routes available. These routes allow you to enjoy the picturesque landscapes without overly strenuous climbs, often mixing secondary roads with access roads to beautiful corners of the area.

Q: Can I find circular road cycling routes in the area?
A: Absolutely. Many of the road cycling routes around Almonaster La Real are circular, allowing you to start and end in the same location. A popular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e991492186/circular-route-through-valdemusa-el-cerro-de" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Circular route through Valdemusa, El Cerro de&lt;/a&gt;, which covers 87.2 km through varied terrain and offers expansive views.

Q: What are some of the most challenging road cycling routes in Almonaster La Real?
A: For experienced cyclists seeking a challenge, the region offers several difficult routes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e991490820/almonaster-la-real-encinasola-barrancos-aroche-cortegana-circular-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Almonaster la Real, Encinasola, Barrancos, Aroche, Cortegana – circular route&lt;/a&gt; is a demanding 108.9 km trail with significant elevation gain. Another challenging option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e991323092/almonaster-la-real-santa-ana-la-real-alajar-castillo-de-cortegana-circular-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Almonaster La Real, Santa Ana La Real, Alájar, Castillo de Cortegana – circular route&lt;/a&gt;, a difficult 83.4 km path winding through varied landscapes and historic sites.

Q: What cultural and historical attractions can I see along the road cycling routes?
A: The region is rich in history. You can encounter unique sites like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4550593" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mosque of Almonaster la Real&lt;/a&gt;, a rare intact rural mosque, and the historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4184233" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Arias Montano Peak and the Hermitage of Our Lady of the Angels&lt;/a&gt;, offering stunning views. The town of Almonaster La Real itself is a declared historic and artistic site with an Arab castle, Mudéjar and Gothic houses, and cobbled streets.

Q: Are there any scenic viewpoints or natural landmarks to look out for?
A: Yes, the routes often provide 'spectacular views of the entire Sierra de Aracena and Picos de Aroche Park,' with some viewpoints making you feel 'like you can almost touch the clouds.' The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4086077" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cerro de San Cristóbal&lt;/a&gt; is known for its panoramic vistas. You might also pass by natural features like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5336413" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Valdelamusa Water-Filled Quarry&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5301124" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Calabazar Reservoir&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Almonaster La Real?
A: The region's climate makes it suitable for cycling for much of the year. Autumn is particularly striking, as the abundant chestnut forests display vibrant colors, contrasting with the evergreen oaks and pines. Spring also offers pleasant temperatures and lush landscapes. Summers can be hot, so early morning or late afternoon rides are advisable during those months.

Q: Are there any routes that pass through natural parks?
A: Yes, many routes traverse the Sierra de Aracena y Picos de Aroche Natural Park, offering immersive experiences in its diverse ecosystems. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e991339161/sierra-pelada-natural-park-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sierra Pelada Natural Park Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a challenging 126 km route that takes you directly through this protected area, showcasing its unique flora and fauna.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about cycling in Almonaster La Real?
A: Road cyclists frequently praise the region for its quiet, scenic roads and the sense of immersion in nature. The varied landscapes, from traditional dehesas to dense chestnut forests, along with the panoramic views of the Sierra, are often highlighted. The mix of challenging climbs and more gentle sections also caters to a wide range of preferences.

Q: Are there any road cycling routes that connect multiple towns or villages?
A: Yes, several routes are designed to connect various towns and villages, allowing you to explore the local culture and architecture. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e991490820/almonaster-la-real-encinasola-barrancos-aroche-cortegana-circular-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Almonaster la Real, Encinasola, Barrancos, Aroche, Cortegana – circular route&lt;/a&gt; is a prime example, linking several communities over a significant distance and elevation.
tips: In recent decades this medieval monument has been appreciated and with good reason! It is the old Muslim mosque, later converted into the Christian Hermitage of the Conception of Our Lady. There are records of its existence for more than 1,700 years, and it is supposed to have been built on top of some monumental Roman building from the 1st or 2nd century AD.

The traditional popular architecture of the area stands out. You must see the parish church of Santa Ana, in a Renaissance style and with a dome and a tower worth visiting, and the town's lime kiln, an old lime kiln that refers to both the culture and history of the town.
Impressive views from the Sierra de Alajar. Many times there is mist or fog, but if everything is clear you can see the Punta Umbría apartment blocks and the sea.

This medieval fortress dates from the 13th century and its origin is still not completely clear, although it can be assumed that due to its shape and type of construction, its character was originally defensive. You can visit the inside and also the views from the path on the outside are very picturesque. For more info visit the official website (https://www.castillodecortegana.net/)
